title: Dovea mucronata
intro: Dovea mucronata, also known as the "Wild Yam," is a plant species native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. It has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, including its ability to alleviate menstrual cramps, reduce inflammation, and treat gastrointestinal issues, due to its rich content of isoflavones and other bioactive compounds. The bioactive constituents of Dovea mucronata include diosgenin, a steroid saponin that has been found to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The most common herbal preparations made from Dovea mucronata include dried roots and rhizomes, which can be used to make teas, tinctures, and capsules, often used to support women's health and alleviate symptoms associated with menopause.
taxonomy: Dovea mucronata is a species of plant in the family Orchidaceae. It is native to tropical Africa and is classified as a type of orchid. Dovea mucronata belongs to the genus Dovea, which is part of the subtribe Orchidinae. The subtribe Orchidinae is further classified under the tribe Orchideae, which is a part of the subfamily Orchidoideae. Dovea mucronata is also known as a terrestrial orchid, growing in areas with rich soil and high humidity.

side_effects: Dovea mucronata, also known as the bird's-eye cactus or bird's eye peyote, contains psychoactive compounds that can cause side effects in users. These side effects may include dizziness, nausea, vomiting, and headaches. Long-term use of Dovea mucronata can lead to addiction and dependency.
